description: Persistent issue tracker. Decider appends new issues from Curmudgeon reviews, marks resolved when wins.json no longer contains the flagged text.

issues.description: VERDICT CATEGORY OVERLOAD: 'Misleading' verdict conflates 4 distinct failure modes: (1) data misrepresentation, (2) duplication/redundancy, (3) non-discrimination (observation explained by multiple models equally), (4) circular derivation. 23 WINs under one label implies systematic dishonesty, but many are simply non-discriminating observations. Consider subcategory 'Non-Discriminating' or move non-discriminating WINs to 'Std Model Explains'. Affects verdict taxonomy coherence and reviewer's interpretation of dome author intent.
issues.rationale_for_deferral: Verdict taxonomy restructuring requires human editorial decision on subcategory definitions

issues.description: V51.1 split sun altitude: physical <4,200 km / apparent 5,733 km. Almost certainly responding to our sun/firmament collision critique. Our argument needs updating to show why the split makes things WORSE: (1) inequality is unfalsifiable, (2) refraction needed is ~15° vs real ~0.01°, (3) if refraction is that extreme, all triangulation measurements including the ones used to derive dome parameters become unreliable. Update the relevant kill-shot section.

V51.1 added Steelman Evidence Ladder (4 tiers) and killshot.html (August 2026 eclipse prediction). Evidence Ladder: Tier 1 (Australia) has 6.2% RMSE vs mm-level geodesy; none of the tiers discriminate dome from globe. Killshot page: Kp<2 precondition voids test ~50% of time; -28.9 nT conflicts with homepage -17 to -21 nT and code -29.1 nT (now THREE different values). Needs analysis section.

V51.1 introduced sub-terrestrial cavity parameter δ = 6,371 km — exactly the mean radius of the globe Earth. Combined with disc radius a = π×R = 20,015 km, both horizontal AND vertical dome scales are globe-derived. This is a smoking-gun fingerprint. Needs integration into the globe fingerprint narrative (Part 2b or wherever the coordinate system discussion lives).

WIN-027's V12 quadratic correction law was SUPERSEDED by V13's H(r) exponential function. Our review critiques the quadratic correction as a post-hoc fudge factor, but the model no longer uses it. We may be attacking a straw man. Need to update evidence to note the V12→V13 evolution and show why V13 H(r) is equally problematic.

Dome explicitly addresses WIN-002/WIN-029 tension in its evolution text: 'Not contradictory. Complementary. The dome medium damps the resonance (WIN-002), and the damped measured frequency still back-derives the correct dome height (WIN-029).' Our review does not quote or rebut this explanation. Need to add rebuttal showing why H(r) supremum of 8,537 km cannot spatial-average to 9,086 km.

LONGITUDE MISMATCH not mentioned: The Roaring 40s wind belt encircles the entire latitude band continuously. Under dome geometry, southern latitudes compress toward the rim — the circumference at 40°S dome latitude is far shorter than on a globe. A continuous circumpolar wind belt is geometrically impossible on a dome disc where the southern hemisphere rim is stretched. This is the strongest refutation and our review omits it.

Missing code-level evidence: 2 of 3 WIN-024 validation domains (Domains 27, 33) hardcode pass=True, making them untestable by the monitoring system. Our review doesn't mention this — it's a code analysis finding that directly undermines the dome's claim of ongoing monitoring of this WIN.

WIN-030 detail_evidence paragraphs 1 and 2 contain substantial redundancy — both make the identical WGS84-projection argument ('inter-city distances computed from WGS84 globe coordinates...ellipse absorbing projection distortion...any smooth closed curve'). Para 2 should be revised to add new information (e.g., AIC/BIC penalization analysis) rather than restating para 1.

WIN-031 rebuttal is too shallow — 2-3 sentences without engaging what the dome actually claims about the Antarctic ice boundary. Need to expand with: what the dome's boundary prediction says, why standard geomagnetic models (IGRF/CHAOS) explain geomagnetic patterns without a dome boundary, and whether any observational test could distinguish the dome boundary claim from standard field models.
